assessor_raw:
## Baseline Assessment: Sequence Models vs. Tabular Baselines for 30-Day Readmission

The design uses a chronological split and tests sequence models against tabular baselines for predicting 30-day unplanned readmission. The split design, preprocessing discipline, and equal tuning effort are appropriate.

**Critical concern:** The design includes as an input feature 'early post-discharge utilization signal' — whether the patient had any outpatient follow-up, ED presentation, nurse triage contact, or medication reconciliation within 7 days of discharge. The prediction target is 30-day readmission; the intended use case is clinical decision support at discharge time. At the moment of discharge, the next 7 days of care utilization have not occurred. Including this as a feature means the model is trained and evaluated on data that includes post-discharge events that would not be available at deployment time. This is temporal leakage. A patient who attends an ED visit within 3 days of discharge is at high readmission risk; that ED visit information was collected post-discharge and should not be available for a discharge-time intervention model. Both models receive this feature, so the model comparison is internally consistent, but neither model reflects what is actually achievable at discharge.
